data.title: RFI: A Look At This Non-Leveraged 8.6% Yielding Real Estate Fund
data.description:
It has been over two years since we last wrote on Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty (RFI). The last time though, we ran it as a comparison against another closed end fund. Today, we examine it on a standalone basis and tell you why this should be kept on your radar.

The Fund

RFI is a closed end fund that aims to provide its investors a high total return courtesy of investments in real estate securities. For those wondering whether this $300 million fund prioritizes income or capital gain, the prospectus comes to the rescue.

The Fund will seek both current income and capital appreciation (realized and unrealized) with approximately equal emphasis.

Source: Prospectus

A company that that falls under the definition of a real estate investment for RFI is one that derives:

... at least 50% of its revenues from the ownership, construction, financing, manage ment or sale of commercial, industrial, or residential real estate or that has at least 50% of its assets in such real estate (such as REITs).

Source: Prospectus

In terms of type of securities, RFI covers the whole spectrum, including common shares, convertibles, preferreds, debt, rights and warrants. RFI also reserves the right to invest up to 10% of its assets in illiquid securities i.e. those "for which there is no readily available secondary market". As of September 30, most of the funds were invested in common stocks with the rest being allocated between preferreds and bonds.

In terms of sector allocations within the real estate spectrum, Healthcare, Telecommunications and corporate bonds comprised a little more than a third of the portfolio. The fund had a host of other sectors in the portfolio, including Data Centers, Industrial, Self Storage, Residential, and Retail.

Counting those in the "Other" category, one would be hard pressed to find a type of real estate sector that RFI did not have its hands in. Individually, Welltower Inc. (WELL) was the top holding, followed by a bunch of familiar names like American Tower Corporation (AMT), Digital Realty Trust Inc (DLR), Crown Castle Inc (CCI), and Prologis (PLD)

We have covered the top 5 holdings of RFI on this platform a few times now. Except for CCI, none of them have received a buy rating from our side.

Distributions

RFI has a managed distribution policy, which means their distributions do not vary in the medium term, irrespective of fund net asset value or income changes. The management might lower the payout if they feel that it is unsustainable over the long run. In the case of our protagonist, the monthly payout has been 8 cents since 2016, with a few special distributions thrown in around year end.

Taking into account the current market price of RFI ($11.21), this fund yields 8.6%.

Expenses

RFI has a management fee of 0.70%, and an overall expense ratio (including management fee) of 0.94%.

Considering that this fund has done quite well without taking on leverage, the fees, in our opinion are acceptable.

Performance

RFI presents a comparison of its performance with FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index (Linked Index) and a Blended Benchmark. The latter comprises 80% FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index and 20% ICE BofA REIT Preferred Securities Index. We will go ahead and ignore the Magnificent Seven driven S&P 500 Index, also shown below. This comparison is probably a throwback to the 2000 - 2020 timeframe when the REITs were crushing the broader market.

RFI has done better than the two real estate focused indices throughout all time frames. The market price has had its fair amount of ups and downs, trading at a discount or premium to its net asset value of its lifetime.

The current NAV is around $11.20 and the market price is almost the same. Based on the premiums it has typically enjoyed over the last year, this fund is in the inexpensive zone.

The above noted Z-Scores also support buying at this price point. However, just because a fund is trading cheaper than it has over the last 12 months, does not automatically make it a buy. If we look at the "Premium/Discount" chart above, there have been long periods where it has been consistently in the discount zone. One has to consider the market conditions to determine whether this is the right time to buy. Yes, of course, the Z-Scores give you an extra edge in picking an entry point, but this is by no means the end all and be all in picking your closed end funds.

Verdict

There are very few CEFs that fall under the "buy and hold" category for us. Most of them take too much leverage for the market valuations we have today. Even amongst the ones that do that do not go "pedal to the metal", and we wrote about one just this week, they tend to underperform the indices. RFI, admittedly, is a different beast. A long history coupled with zero leverage is married here with some outperformance. Granted, that when you run the numbers from Vanguard Real Estate ETF's (VNQ) start date, it isn't that much.

But this is still very impressive for three reasons. First, RFI has a way higher expense ratio than the bare bones VNQ ETF. The latter just charges 0.13%. The second hurdle is that RFI distributes a lot compared to VNQ. VNQ pays out what the underlying companies distribute. Whereas RFI, as noted above, has a managed distribution policy. This makes it much harder for RFI to outperform as it is forced to sell assets at every point and sell more assets when prices are lower. Finally, RFI also holds some lower volatility preferred shares and corporate bonds. These obviously don't appreciate as much in up cycles and have actually had all the levity of lead balloons during 2022-2023. Overall, this fund is an interesting one to keep on your watch list to pick up real estate exposure. We certainly prefer it over chronic horrid performers like abrdn Global Premier Property (AWP).

We even generally prefer it over its brethren Closed End Funds from the same management company as they use leverage. At present, we still like the idea of using an individual stock covered call approach where we can combine the right price and right covered call to generate high income. RFI gets a "hold" from us.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: Not Passing The Test, After Buying Test Maker Exact Sciences
data.description:
Shares of Abbott Laboratories (ABT) have seen a small negative price reaction in response to a larger deal for its smaller diagnostic peer Exact Sciences (EXAS), taking place at a revenue premium on the back of stronger growth, although Exact still has to reach profitability.

The debt and dilution incurred outweighs the added growth profile contribution and while I am usually appealed by setbacks in the share price, I am not seeing enough of a compelling situation here. This prevents me from buying this dip just yet.

Life. To The Fullest

The paragraph header is the mission of Abbott, which aims to reimagine innovation for both a better and healthier world. The company focuses on technologies which allow for accessible and affordable care, helping to live healthier and better lives.

I previously wrote about Abbott here, but for those who are unfamiliar, here is a background: the company is a true giant, employing over 110,000 workers in over 160 countries, with its history dating back to the late 19th century. The company is a huge business, generating over $40 billion, close to $42 billion to be precise.

This is truly a global business with some 60% of sales generated abroad and this huge revenue base being generated across four main segments. The largest of these is a $19 billion medical device segment, coincidentally also being the fastest grower here. This includes activities like rhythm management, hearth failure, vascular, neuromodulation and diabetes care.

This is complemented by a >$9 billion diagnostic segment, a >$8 billion nutrition business and an over $5 billion pharmaceutical business. These activities include molecular services, point of care, rapid diagnostics, pediatric and adult nutrition, etc.

Because of these widespread activities, the business is quite diversified as Abbott has a decent growth track record, in between organic and M&A achievements. Over the past decade, the company has doubled sales, grown margins, while dilution has been limited to about 15% over this period of time.

This has been well reflected in the stock, which has essentially tripled from $40 to $120 over his period of time, as shares already hit these levels in 2021. Ever since, shares have traded in a $100-$140 range over the past five years.

Shedding Perspective

In January of this year, Abbott reported a near 5% increase in reported sales to $42 billion, with underlying revenue growth reported around 9% offset by adverse currency moves. Adjusted net earnings were reported at $8.2 billion, equal to $4.67 per share with that number up twenty-three cents on the year before, as that metric looked quite fair.

The company issued quite an upbeat tone for 2025, seeing organic sales up between 7.5% and 8.5%, with earnings seen up to $5.05-$5.25 per share. An important part of these earnings are paid out to investors, with the dividends being paid out consecutively for over a century. Dividends have been hiked for 53 years in a row, with payouts at the time standing at $0.59 per share per quarter. Largely trading around the $120 mark, shares traded at demanding valuations, at around 23–24 times forward adjusted earnings.

Throughout the year, the company has largely delivered on its expectations, with the full year organic sales guidance for the year now seen at 7.5-8.0%, as of the third quarter, with adjusted earnings still seen at a midpoint of $5.15 per share. A share count of 1.75 billion shares translates into an equity valuation of $219 billion at $125 per share, this including a modest net debt load of around $5 billion.

A resulting $224 billion enterprise valuation values the business at around 5 times sales, with earnings trading at 24 times anticipated earnings.

A Big Deal

Trying to grow the diversified health business even more, Abbott announced the purchase of Exact Sciences for $105 per share, valuing its equity at $21 billion. Exact’s early cancer diagnostics test, including its Cologuard and Oncodetect business, are seen contributing greatly to Abbott's diagnostics business.

Including net debt of $1.8 billion, the total transaction terms call for a nearly $23 billion deal and with revenues seen over $3 billion this year, sales multiples come in just over 7 times sales, however these revenues are increasing at a high teens organic growth rate.

In fact, the deal presentation revealed revenues are seen at $3.2 billion, derived from over 5 million tests performed each year, and while the sales multiple comes at a premium, the overall impact to Abbott's organic growth rate overall is about 50 basis points.

With Exact Sciences barely breaking even on an operating basis here, the deal is expected to have a twenty cent dilutive impact to 2026 earnings per share, with accretion seen from 2028 and beyond. This is driven by organic growth and a targeted $100 million in cost synergies. With pro forma net debt seen moving up to $28 billion, the company sees pro forma net debt around 2.7 times EBITDA.

And Now?

Shares of Abbott fell from $129 to $125 per share upon the news announcement, with shares down $7 billion on the back of the premium offered in this $23 billion deal. This decline coincides largely with the premium offered for the shares of Exact, after shares traded in the $60s ahead of takeover rumors.

In between all this, I think that the reaction to the deal looks quite fair. Right now Abbott has been taking on quite some leverage and while the payout ratio is relatively high, solid organic growth is impressive enough as I do not have leverage concerns.

In between all this, the reaction looks largely fair, with the premium reflected in a fall in Abbott's shares, in part as leverage will jump, but also as earnings multiples will increase from 24 times to 25 times was well, despite the lower share price, as the earnings estimates have been cut quite a bit following dilution incurred.

In between all this, the reaction looks largely fair. While Abbott has proven to be a solid long term value creator, expectations remain a bit elevated even as shares have been making inroads, after trading flat for years.

To see appeal, I need to see a slightly smaller premium attached to the shares, i.e. a long period of share price stagnation, or moves closer to the $100 mark, before getting appealed to the long term (dividend) value creator.

data.title: RFMZ: Best Value Of The Lineup
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

RiverNorth offers a lineup of municipal bond-focused closed-end funds; these take an approach of investing in both individual bonds and other muni-focused CEFs. The strategy is an attempt to trade around based on the overall environment for CEF investing. These generally aren't focused on providing high levels of tax-free distributions, though that is a part of the distribution characteristic naturally from investing in munis. The classification that gets the largest allocation is often nondividend distributions (aka return of capital).

That can come with its own potential tax benefits, but it is not the same as federally and state tax-exempt distributions that some investors may be looking for. That's why I've continued to believe that these funds are better for traders and not a focus for investors wanting tax-free distributions from muni exposure.

The one fund that is currently trading at the widest discount for this basket of funds is the RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income II (RFMZ), making it look like the more attractive option at the moment. However, RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income (RFM) and RiverNorth Opportunity Municipal Income Fund (RMI) also aren't too far behind in terms of absolute discount.

RFMZ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.02
  • Discount/Premium: -10.98%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.03%
  • Expense Ratio: 2.38%
  • Leverage: 39.9%
  • Managed Assets: $593.8 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date Feb. 26, 2036)

RFMZ's investment objective is "to provide current income exempt from regular U.S. federal income taxes, with a secondary objective of total return."

In an attempt to achieve this, the fund will "allocate the Fund's managed assets between two principal strategies: Tactical Municipal Closed-End Fund Strategy (managed by RiverNorth) and Municipal Bond Income Strategy (managed by MacKay Shields)."

In determining the allocation, it is by the CEF environment. Below is a rough idea of how the allocations will work at any given time.

With RFMZ, the fund is highly leveraged, and that isn't all that unusual for a muni CEF—after all, muni bonds are extremely safe.

That said, the underlying CEFs the fund will invest in can also be leveraged highly. So that is leverage on leverage, as well as fees on fees, and also potential discount on discount volatility. That can make these funds quite risky and volatile compared to traditional muni exposure or even holding a muni-focused CEF on its own.

For RFMZ, when including borrowing costs for its own leverage, the fund's total expense ratio comes up to 4.75%. That has eased from the 5.08% in the prior fiscal year on the back of Fed rate cuts.

Performance - Widest Discount of Peers

Since our last update, the fund has delivered positive total returns. It had underperformed the S&P 500 Index, but that wouldn't be an appropriate benchmark given the muni focus of RFMZ.

When looking at the total returns of RFMZ over the longer term compared to its sister funds, on a total share price basis, it comes in second place. That is just behind the RiverNorth Managed Duration Municipal Income Fund II (RMMZ).

(Note: RMM is not included in the chart because Ycharts is missing the data.)

We are limited in the timeline of looking at historical performance by RMMZ, which is the last of these funds that were launched. The fund's inception date is February 10, 2022. While the total NAV returns of the funds are quite close, RMMZ has come out ahead on that front as well.

That is also the one that carries the narrowest discount—which could suggest investors believe that outperformance could continue. With that, investors could question whether they think that outperformance is going to continue and if that makes up for the differential in the valuation between the two funds. These funds are highly correlated, and RMMZ has not pulled away enough historically for me to consider it a slam dunk win.

(Note: RMM's discount sits at 8.11%.)

On another note, RFMZ and RMMZ are both term-structured funds where they are anticipated to be liquidated in the future. Alternatively, and what is more likely, they will go through a tender offer first in an attempt to go perpetual. After the tender offer, if there are at least $100 million in net assets, the fund can remove its term structure and convert to a perpetual fund. In the case of RFMZ, the term date is Feb. 26, 2036. That's well off from now and isn't too material to consider for the time being.

Beginning one year before February 26, 2036 (the "Termination Date"), the Fund may begin to liquidate all or a portion of the Fund's portfolio. At the discretion of the Board of Directors ("Board"), the Termination Date may be extended (i) once for up to one year, and (ii) once for up to an additional six months; or Within twelve months prior to the Termination Date, the Fund may conduct a tender offer to purchase 100% of the then outstanding shares. Following the completion of the tender offer, the Fund must have at least $100 million of net assets. The Board may then eliminate the Termination Date and convert the Fund to a perpetual structure upon the affirmative vote of a majority of the Board.

Managed Distribution Provides Elevated Payout

RFMZ currently maintains a 6.75% NAV distribution rate that is reset annually. However, they have made adjustments to this level in the past, so this payout isn't necessarily set in stone.

At this time, the NAV rate comes to 7.15%, so that would indicate a cut will be coming for next year unless they change the rate for the policy or we see some very strong performance in the last month or so of the year.

In general, the fund is banking on capital gains to fund its distribution, which would come from trading its underlying muni CEFs. In the last fiscal year that ended June 30, 2025, the fund had net investment income of $0.40. That can be compared to the fund paying out $1.03 in total distributions. During the fiscal year, the fund also had unrealized/realized losses of $0.99 per share.

On a more positive note, in a lower rate environment, that can help to ease borrowing costs on the short side, and if long-term rates come down, that can also help push underlying bond prices higher. RFMZ is leveraged, and its underlying CEF holdings are also often leveraged, making lower borrowing costs even more impactful for the fund. With that, we have been and are continuing to be in a more favorable rate environment for the fund to operate.

However, as noted about the NAV rate being elevated compared to its managed policy level set at the start of the year, it wasn't enough to cover the payout. Beyond overpaying for the distribution, the fund's deep discount also helps to elevate the market distribution rate even higher—pushing it to 8.03% currently.

Given the lack of distribution coverage and capital gains to cover the payout, it isn't too surprising that we see a heavier classification of the distribution as return of capital.

Generally, ROC is often a very misunderstood thing when it comes to CEFs, as some investors assume it is automatically a negative. There are cases where ROC distributions can show up, but the fund's NAV is still rising or staying flat.

In this case, as the NAV has been trending lower, it would indicate destructive ROC. Given the elevated rate that the fund is attempting to pay as well, it seems that there is likely always to be some destructive ROC. RMM was a fund that existed in 2020 and 2021, when the Fed funds rate was pegged at or near zero. Even during that time, the fund was still paying a distribution level over what it could generate in NII.

However, to be fair, most of the destruction came shortly after the initial launch when the Fed was raising its benchmark rate aggressively. Given that muni bonds are extremely interest rate sensitive, with RFMZ's estimated unhedged duration at 11.3 years, and long rates were also rising, the hit makes sense. With the estimated hedged duration, it comes down to 7.6 years, but that is still meaningful. With that, the erosion over the last few years has been relatively minimal compared to that.

RFMZ's Portfolio

At this time, the fund's strategy is weighted more toward the individual bond income strategy over the CEF sleeve. Within the CEF portfolio, the weighted average discount comes to -6.6%. That would be where the discount on discount comes from, as RFMZ is already trading at a 10%+ discount level too. As expected, we can also see that most of the portfolio is listed at investment grade.

The largest state exposure that the fund carries is to New York, California, and Illinois. This is generally the case, given that these are the states with some of the highest GDP and, thus, the states that issue the most.

When looking at the top positions, Seeking Alpha notes that it is quite concentrated, comprising around 43%. Of course, that isn't necessarily that bad when you also have that fund of funds sleeve within the portfolio. In fact, the largest holding is the Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD), which itself has over 1000 underlying positions.

Conclusion

RFMZ is an interesting fund given its deep discount, which can make it the best of its current sister funds that all invest rather similarly. Historically, the funds tend to have high correlation, though RMMZ did get a bit of a boost in total returns relative to the rest of the lineup. However, the biggest "boost" was in the form of the fund's total share price returns, thanks to the fund seeing its discount narrow to the shallowest level. On a total NAV return basis, the gap is much less material.

That said, I don't view these funds as great ways to gain muni exposure for investors seeking tax-exempt distributions, as they are more trading vehicles. They've also historically provided a minority of the distribution being classified as tax-exempt; rather, return of capital has been the largest classification.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 4 Reasons Stocks Plunged And "The Big Buy Indicator" That Was Just Triggered
data.description:
The S&P 500 Index (SPY) and other major indexes have dropped for the past few days, and the losses are starting to add up, with the major indexes now down between 5% and 8% from the recent highs. I have been bearish on stocks for a while now, and over two weeks ago I wrote this article suggesting that all the ingredients we needed for a stock market correction were present and that I believed it would happen soon. Now that we have had a 5% to 8% decline in some major indexes, I believe the market is in a healthier position, and this reset could actually help fuel a year-end "Santa Claus Rally" which I just wrote about in this article. This market plunge seems to have taken some investors off guard, so I want to review the reasons why it happened and also why it might be smart to do some selective buying in anticipation of a year-end rally. With this in mind, let's take a closer look at the sell-off:

What Sparked The Sell Off

I see 4 major reasons for the recent market plunge, and this includes: the Fed, froth, Japan, and Bitcoin. In recent days, bond yields in Japan have jumped, and this is causing stress for some global investors who have used the "Yen Carry Trade" over the years, whereby they borrow at low rates in Japan and deploy the cash elsewhere. The rise in Japanese interest rates has spooked some investors and caused some selling pressure on certain global assets. This situation with Japanese rates was already causing some market stress, but then on November 19, the Federal Reserve released minutes that showed a rate cut in December is not a sure thing. This appeared to trigger more concerns in the equity market and in particular with Bitcoin, which saw a renewed plunge shortly after investors realized the Fed might not cut rates at the next meeting, which is on December 9 and 10.

In terms of speculative froth and Bitcoin, which go hand in hand in my opinion, there was just too much of it. Seeing high-flying tech stocks and meme stocks surge in recent weeks was one sign of speculative excess. But it was also obvious that some investors were going far out on a limb when you see how much margin debt has been rising. Margin debt recently rose to an all-time record of around $1.2 trillion. In my opinion, and as I warned in some of my recent articles, this level of borrowing was the perfect setup for a market correction because that has historically been the case when margin debt gets this high. High levels of margin debt and speculation in assets like Bitcoin show investors have become complacent, and complacency is typically present just before a market decline.

Nvidia Is Not The Problem, But Bitcoin Still Might Be

Nvidia (NVDA) released a stellar Q3 earnings report and solid guidance on November 19th. This initially sparked a rebound in tech stocks and the market in general. However, on the morning of November 20th, even though the stock market was enjoying a strong rally, Bitcoin started to turn lower. There have been reports of margin calls with many accounts that hold Bitcoin, and that seems to be putting forced selling pressure on the cryptocurrency sector, which has been dropping for weeks now. Bitcoin hit a record high in October of about $125,000, and it has been downhill almost ever since. I believe Bitcoin holders also frequently own popular tech stocks like Nvidia, and many of them might have been forced to sell their tech stocks in order to shore up their portfolios and reduce risks.

Bitcoin has become a leveraged "asset" and it could continue to decline and put more selling pressure on the stock market. This recent article suggests significant margin call selling has hit the cryptocurrency markets, and it mentions that Coinbase (COIN) and other exchanges have enabled leveraged bets on Bitcoin, which are now being unwound, it states:

"This expansion of leverage has accelerated risk transmission. Traders can now gain exposure of $100 in Bitcoin with only $1 of collateral. When markets move downward, losses are magnified, positions breach margin thresholds, and holdings are forcibly liquidated. As Nansen research analyst Nicolai Søndergaard notes, investors may have more ways to amplify returns, but poor risk management now carries sharper consequences."

I believe Bitcoin continues to pose a risk to the financial markets as it is hard to know how much margin call selling might be left and also how much worse the decline might get. As shown in the chart below, Bitcoin is now approaching the lows it hit in April when the Liberation Day tariff announcement roiled the markets. This suggests there could be more downside ahead for Bitcoin, but it is very oversold now, so we might see some investors with laser eyes step in to buy this dip.

Where Things Stand Technically

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is now about 5% below the recent all-time highs, although other indexes are off even more. Many investors have been rewarded by buying the dip in recent years, and for some, a 5% pullback is where they start buying. Because of this, we might see the market stabilize around current levels, especially if Bitcoin stabilizes as well. The S&P 500 Index is now trading below the 50-day moving average, which is represented by the blue trend line on the chart. The 200-day moving average is represented by the red trend line, and reaching this level would represent an additional decline of about 7%, which I do not see as being likely at this time.

Here's The "Big Buy Indicator" That I See

The CNN Fear & Greed Index is an indicator that I follow because I have seen it work as a contrarian indicator. For example, when the index is in the greed zone, I view this as a time to consider selling stocks, and when it is at extreme fear levels, I force myself to do some buying because asset prices are typically "on sale" when investors are fearful. As shown below, this index is at just 6 right now. This is indicating extreme fear levels, and this is historically a buy signal for contrarian investors. When other investors are this fearful, I want to be buying stocks.

Reasons For Optimism And To Start Buying

This decline in the stock market has been rapid, and this could just be a blip and a healthy reset that cleanses the market of some of the excesses. Stocks are now oversold and could be due for a bounce for technical reasons as well.

This decline could now set the market up for a year-end Santa Claus rally, which history shows is often the case.

I believe the Fed will cut in January if it does not cut in December. After this sell-off, the Fed might feel more pressure to cut rates and feel better about it since some of the froth and excess has just been wrung out of the market and out of Bitcoin. The odds suggest that there is a 66% chance of a Fed rate cut in January, and if investors start to focus on that, it could set the stage for a year-end rally.

Some stocks have been beaten down due to tax-loss selling and, in some cases just took another hit with this broad market sell off. This has created some bargain buying opportunities and tax-loss selling is going to fade over the next few weeks as the end of the year rapidly approaches. These beaten-down stocks could be poised to rebound sharply and help lift the stock market.

Thanksgiving week is typically a strong one for stocks. History shows that the S&P 500 Index typically rises 75% of the time, with a gain of nearly 1% on average.

What I Am Focused On Buying Now

At this time of year I usually focus on buying stocks that appear to be getting hit with tax-loss selling. This includes stocks like Whirlpool (WHR) which hedge fund legend David Tepper recently announced a very major stake in. I am also buying stocks like Home Depot (HD), Centene (CNC) and Calavo Growers (CVGW). In terms of tech stocks, Amazon (AMZN) is now on the top of my buy list since it just reported a strong Q3, and yet the stock gave up its recent gains which makes it very attractive to me. I see the sell-off in stocks like Amazon as being unwarranted and I believe Amazon shares can rebound into Thanksgiving week as investors focus on the massive amounts of money being spent by holiday shoppers on "Black Friday" and the upcoming holiday season.

Potential Risks To Consider

The amount of leverage in Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies has grown large and since this is a relatively new "asset" class and some of the financial products within it have not been tested extensively, so the sell-off could get worse. Margin call selling might not be done yet when it comes to Bitcoin or stocks. Japan remains a risk worth considering, as interest rates in that country could continue to move higher. However, the economy in Japan is showing strong growth, so interest rates are rising for a good reason.

In Summary

I have been bearish recently as I expected a market sell-off; however, now that we have seen a lot of speculative assets decline significantly, I think the market is looking healthier since some froth has been removed. I believe this sell-off makes it easier for the Fed to lower interest rates in either December or January. I also believe that since this time of year is typically strong for stocks, this reset in valuations makes a year-end rally more likely. As a contrarian investor, I see the extreme fear in the market as a time to start doing some buying, especially stocks like Amazon.

data.title: The Quiet Forces Driving This Correction
data.description:
Market Outlook

Markets S&P 500 (SP500) (SPX) (SPY) (VOO) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) are entering a fragile phase defined by valuation fatigue, policy uncertainty, and a shifting global liquidity backdrop. Following my earlier market outlook, we now have both the Nvidia (NVDA) earnings reaction and the latest labor-market report, two critical components I highlighted, yet the focus has already shifted toward global liquidity and yen-driven volatility.

Nvidia’s sell-off despite strong results reflects cooling AI enthusiasm, while mixed jobs data cloud expectations for Fed cuts. At the same time, Japan’s tightening and a stronger yen threaten to unwind crowded carry trades. With thinning liquidity and algorithms amplifying shocks, markets appear closer to a bottoming process than a sustained downtrend.

The Labor Market and the Fed's Dilemma

The jobs figures in October had mixed messages. The initial jobs figure was robust and better than market expectations, although job quality was not particularly convincing. Full-time employment has not shown any significant recent improvement, and part-time and flexible employment trends are responsible for accommodating most of the changes in the workforce.

Meanwhile, the pace of wage growth has declined, at least on a year-over-year basis, so there is less bargaining power despite the increased level of hiring. The participation rate has held steady, rather than increasing appreciably, to support views of a labor market that appears to be growing but is shrinking at its core.

Markets caught up. The chance of a December cut, already solidly above 75%, dipped below 50% within a few days. Interest rate outlooks shape stock market values, particularly in long-duration industries such as technology. Software, semiconductors, and high-multiple AI equities need stable and decreasing discount rates to support their respective models.

But with the Fed weighing premature versus delayed actions, "Higher For Longer" scenarios suddenly seem possible again. This is because investors understand that premature easing could reignite worries about inflation, and delayed easing could slow economic growth by more than policymakers desire. As markets reprice such uncertainties, sectors vulnerable to forward earnings, tech, growth, and cryptocurrencies tend to suffer first.

The Yen and Carry Trade Reversals

Beyond the turmoil at home, international events are trending in ways that impact US risk assets directly. The Japanese policy shift is quietly potent. After so many years of yield curve management and negative interest rates, the Bank of Japan has started its normalization process. The marginal change, rate hikes, and signals of further tightening hold high significance, as they have negative implications for the Yen, which is perhaps the world's most influential funding currency.

The yen has appreciated substantially over the past few weeks, and today the USD/JPY is traded at levels just below 157, which has triggered interventions by Japanese authorities in prior instances. Below this level, Japan sells dollars and buys yen to stem further depreciation. The tie to US markets is straightforward, as a stronger yen reverses leveraged carry trades.

For over ten years, international investors borrowed money at nearly zero interest to purchase other, higher-return investments, tech stocks in the US, cryptocurrency, and emerging markets. This type of trade is sound when the Yen is depreciating or in a stable market. The problem arises if the Yen appreciates, making this type of investment not only unprofitable but also ruinous. One must pay back one's Yen-denominated debts. The only method of payment is by selling off Risk Assets.

But this is not a one-off pressure point; it's a blueprint for what's to come. The normalization of Japan's monetary authority indicates that it's prepared to move away from its position as the world's least expensive lender. As long as the Fed keeps interest rates constant and starts to ease, and Japan keeps moving forward with its, at least mild, tightening cycle, the differential decreases. This works to raise, not decrease, the value of the yen. Additionally, it puts pressure on Japanese exporters and causes Japanese institutions to bring home capital. The hedge funds, seeing increased borrowing costs, start to reduce their leverage.

We are entering an era, and that era is one in which you can't simply apply the old game of borrowing in yen and buying growth. This process is slow until it speeds up and then impacts all things from Nvidia to Bitcoin. This is far more than one market reaction. The prospect of additional, even gradual, interest-rate hikes from the Bank of Japan signals a shift away from its long-standing role as the world’s de facto source of ultra-cheap funding.

The Three Roads Ahead: Stability, Shock, or Surge

Markets are set to move into a crucial area, with outcomes at least dependent upon a handful of macro variables, and with the yen emerging as the game-changer. After experiencing such high levels of technology, cryptocurrency, and long-term re-pricing, three pathways emerge, each with a varied pattern of what happens with the Japanese Yen, Fed's timing, and market liquidity. All three pathways will influence the market performance of various risk assets in early 2026.

The next phase will be driven by what happens with the Yen and its interaction with global liquidity. In what represents the prime, most probable course, or "Orderly Stabilization", USD/JPY will be seen to remain below the 157-160 range, which over time has maintained itself below Japan's main intervention level. Noting this potential stabilization and assuming an absence of Fed interest rate hikes before 2026, markets can consolidate. This would be accompanied by a recession threat, a pause in technology share price declines, stabilization at last in cryptocurrency, and establishment of a long-term support structure by Bitcoin.

The second scenario, "Volatility Spike / Hard Yen Intervention", has far more dramatic consequences. If USD/JPY breaks above the danger zone, Japan will probably intervene to support its currency, and this would trigger a strong exchange-rate rise, which would see global yen-funded carry trades reversed sharply. As this process evolves, market participants must sell risk assets such as US equities and cryptocurrency to satisfy yen liabilities. The price of Bitcoin may decline by a further 10-20% and Ethereum's price may drop by more, as it is more sensitive to liquidity. Although this scenario would be quite uncomfortable, it may offer excellent long-term entry points.

The least probable but most volatile case is "Fed Capitulation / Early Cut". The Fed may be pressured into an early interest rate cut if there is weakness in the labor market, and this will inject liquidity into the market. This will ensure that the value of the Japanese Yen is stabilized, thus enhancing financial conditions. The price of Bitcoin and Ethereum will amplify as AI and Semiconductors influence the market's revival.

Where We Stand Now

Today, markets are caught between the first two scenarios. The wild card is the Yen. Yen entering the Intervention Area means market turmoil. But staying disciplined, we could be at or near a tech and crypto cycle low. Regardless, we are closer to a macro cycle bottom than we are to a macro cycle peak.

The overall picture improves, not deteriorates, for long-term investors with a 12+ month horizon. This is not a time to be panicked; it is time to prepare. Quality, hedging, selectivity, and liquidity will be what distinguish those individuals and firms that make this transition smoothly from those that don't. Markets are not broken, but they are fragile. One must respect signals and ignore noise.

data.title: CEVA: Reminds Everyone Of The Downsides Of Backing It
data.description:
CEVA (CEVA), a licensor of IP for wireless connectivity technologies, smart sensing, and other solutions, could have used a good Q3 FY2025 report with its stock stalling after a run lasting six weeks or so, but the stock has fallen off in the days following its release. For a moment, it looked like the stock would regain its balance at a trendline that had provided support since the 2025 low, only to fall off a cliff after CEVA reminded the market of some of its less desirable attributes, which include diluting the stakes of existing shareholders. Why will it be covered next.

CEVA may have opened the door to new lows

A previous article from last August pointed out how CEVA had yet to recoup most of the losses stemming from the selloff following the tariffs turmoil in March/April. This stood out since most stocks, and the indexes in general, were able to recover from tariff-related losses, and many even managed to put it all behind them by trading even higher than where they were prior to the tariffs. This weakness was worth noting down, especially since it left open the possibility CEVA might experience further downside, especially if tariff-related disputes popped up again. In the end, I chose to rate CEVA a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the stock was initially able to trade higher by putting together a rally in September/October, which resulted in a high of $30.88 in mid-October, only to eventually run out of steam. The stock was going sideways prior to the release of the Q3 FY2025 report on November 10, which argued for a catalyst to inject life into the stock, but the report failed to give CEVA a boost because the stock sold off in the following days.

This selloff brought the stock down to the lower trendline, which is formed by connecting all the lows following the 2025 low of $18.31, as shown in the chart. The stock had not fallen below this trendline when it came close to it on prior occasions, and for a while it looked like this was going to happen again after the stock closed at $23.58 on November 17, and as low as $23.35 on an intraday basis, or just above the trendline.

Not only did the trendline provide support, but the stock stayed above a Fibonacci retracement level. The stock went from a low of $18.31 in May to a high of $30.88 in October, and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $18.31 to $30.88 is $23.11, or $0.24 below $23.35. Combine the aforementioned trendline and the Fibonacci retracement with the stock staying above both, and it looked like CEVA had regained its footing after the selloff of the past several days.

How CEVA lost support

CEVA was in position for a rebound on the back of support from a Fibonacci retracement and a trendline, but the stock was able to blow past both after CEVA announced it intends to raise capital with the sale of three million shares, which triggered a 14.5% drop in the price of the stock on November 18. To put this number in perspective, the total number of shares outstanding is currently at 24,017K. CEVA has also increased the share count in each of the past five years, though not by as much as with the latest sale, since the number of shares stood at 22.1M at the end of 2020.

The market gave a thumbs down to the prospect of existing shareholders seeing their stake in CEVA diluted, which not only caused the stock to fall below the support provided by the aforementioned 61.8% Fibonacci retracement, but also the next, or 76.4% Fibonacci retracement of $18.31 to $30.88, or $23.11 and $21.28, respectively, after closing at $20.16 on November 18. It also led to a decisive break below the previously mentioned trendline that had been in effect for the last six months. All this suggests a trip back towards the 52-week or May low of $18.31 is possible since there are no more possible support levels from Fibonacci retracements standing in the way.

Why the latest report from CEVA came in mixed

CEVA has lost 23% of its market cap since the release of the most recent or Q3 FY2025, report on November 10. The proposed share issuance contributed most to the decline, but the post-earnings decline still occurred after a report that was a mixed bag, even though it was able to surpass expectations in some areas. For instance, CEVA reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.11, or $0.01 more than expected, on revenue of $28.38M in Q3 FY2025.

Royalties increased by 6% YoY to $12.4M and licensing increased by 3% YoY for the remaining $16M. In terms of shipments, CEVA reported that a total of 579M units were shipped using IP from CEVA, which includes 303M Bluetooth devices. CEVA also announced Microchip (MCHP) decided to license IP from CEVA. On the other hand, CEVA earned $0.14 a share on revenue of $27.21M in Q3 FY2024.

In terms of GAAP, CEVA posted a net loss of $2.5M, or $0.10 a share, worse than the loss of $1.3M, or $0.06 a year ago. The main difference between the GAAP and non-GAAP number is that the latter leaves out equity-based compensation expense to the tune of $0.19 a share. CEVA finished the quarter with cash, cash equivalents, marketable securities and short-term bank deposits totaling $152.06M, partially offset by $3.78M in debt.

Quarterly guidance calls for Q4 FY2025 revenue of $29-33M, mostly up from $29.22M in Q4 FY2024, and gross margins are expected to be similar to the preceding Q3 FY2025, or 88% on a GAAP basis and 89% on a non-GAAP basis. Using these guidelines from CEVA, my estimate is that non-GAAP EPS will come in at $0.19 a share in Q4 FY2025. Add that to the $0.24 earned in the first three quarters, and non-GAAP EPS is projected to end up at $0.43 in FY2025. Keep in mind that the second half and the last quarter of the fiscal year in particular benefit from seasonality.

In comparison, non-GAAP EPS reached $0.36 in FY2024, which implies a YoY increase of 19.4%. This would be compatible with the double-digit increase in earnings CEVA targeted earlier in the year. FY2025 revenue is projected to end up at $109.31M since Q1-Q3 FY2025 revenue was $78.31M, and assuming revenue of $31M in Q4 FY2025, the midpoint.

CEVA is growing, but arguably not fast enough

Earnings growth of nearly 20% is not bad, but it is arguably disappointing for a couple of reasons. A big reason, if not the main reason, why the stock put together a rally in late 2024 and early 2025 was because Apple (AAPL) decided to incorporate CEVA’s IP in its in-house cellular modem design to be used in devices like the iPhone. On paper, this has the potential to greatly boost royalty payments to CEVA since Apple produces a large number of devices, mostly at the high end.

CEVA also gave a fairly wide range for its FY2025 earnings outlook by calling for double-digit growth. This can mean anything starting at 10% and above. FY2025 EPS is likely to come in at the earlier estimate of $0.43, or an increase of 19.4% YoY, which is a double-digit increase, but it is arguably underwhelming considering CEVA was able to grow non-GAAP EPS from $0.10 in FY2023 to $0.36 in FY2024, an increase of $0.26 a share, or much more than the more recent $0.07 likely to be achieved in FY2025.

Keep in mind that last year’s performance was achieved before the Apple win, yet FY2025 is expected to post smaller gains than FY2024, even with Apple in the mix. It’s true Apple has yet to use its modem in all its devices, and there is room for the number to increase in the coming years as more devices get the modem.

Nevertheless, the difference made seems to be rather modest compared to what was probably expected earlier in the year, especially since CEVA’s outlook of double-digit growth left open the possibility of much higher growth than nearly 20%, like 30%, 40%, 50%, or even higher. This probably contributed to the recent swoon in the stock price.

Why valuations could drive the stock price even lower

It is worth mentioning that CEVA is now oversold. Yet while there may be a bounce due to being oversold, a further slide is not impossible with valuations where they are, in addition to what the charts suggested earlier. For instance, the stock closed at $20.16 on November 18, which means CEVA trades at 46.9x FY2025 EPS using the estimate of $0.43 in non-GAAP EPS.

This is much more that than what most stocks in the sector go for with the median at 23.5x, but paying a higher multiple can still be worth it as long as earnings grow fast enough in relation. For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, if CEVA can grow EPS at a CAGR of 46.9% in the next 3-5 years, then CEVA can be deemed fairly valued relative to growth.

To hit a CAGR of 46.9%, CEVA would need to reach non-GAAP EPS of $1.36 in FY2028, using the estimated EPS of $0.43 for FY2025 as the base. This means CEVA needs to do much better than the almost 20% earnings growth projected for FY2025. It is also much faster than what CEVA was able to do in the past 3-5 years.

The task becomes even harder if CEVA greatly increases the number of outstanding shares like the pending sales of 3M shares since that will weigh on EPS gains. It’s not impossible for CEVA to do better in the future, but if earnings keep growing at the current pace, the stock can be viewed as overvalued.

Investor takeaways

There is still more than a month left in the year, but CEVA is on pace to end 2025 with steep losses in terms of the stock. CEVA actually got off to a good start in 2025 with a rally, especially as expectations rose of what a difference Apple could be for CEVA, but the stock has fallen off as the year went by. CEVA never truly recovered from the selloff induced by the dispute about tariffs, which is probably the consequence of CEVA being very exposed to global trade and China.

The charts suggest that while there may be a short-term bounce with the stock oversold, the stock is heading for a retest of the May low after the stock got hit by a post-earnings report selloff and news CEVA intends to greatly increase the number of outstanding shares with a sale in the millions. While the Q3 FY2025 results and Q4 FY2025 guidance show CEVA is growing, it is arguably not fast enough to warrant present valuations with CEVA trading at a premium to most stocks, as detailed earlier.

I am therefore neutral on CEVA with everything above in mind. CEVA has already dropped a lot in the past several days, and a temporary bounce is possible, but there is also reason to believe the stock can go even lower, possibly even below the 52-week low set in May. Both the charts and valuations point to a stock going lower. Chances are that is what the stock is going to do.

data.title: It's Not Exciting... It's Just Really, Really Good: Agree Realty
data.description:
Agree Realty (ADC) is a net lease REIT that continues to have steady quarters. This kind of consistency is exactly what you would expect from a disciplined company with tenants such as Lowe’s (LOW), Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), and many other recognizable names. Results this quarter came in a bit ahead of expectations, and guidance was nudged higher - again. Having a strong quarter isn’t unusual for ADC, but it reinforces their long-term consistency for investors. A quote from Joey Agree, CEO of Agree Realty:

I'm pleased to report another very strong quarter at ADC as we further expanded and strengthened what we view to be the nation's leading retail portfolio. The unmatched value proposition of our three-pronged approach continues to drive a compelling opportunity set and expansive pipelines across all platforms. We achieved our largest quarterly investment volume since the depth of COVID, five years ago, deploying over $450 million across all three platforms while maintaining a high level of discipline in our underwriting process.

FFO and AFFO

  • Core FFO: $1.09 per share

  • AFFO: $1.10 per share

Both of these metrics exceeded consensus estimates by $0.02. ADC raised guidance for full-year 2025 AFFO per share:

  • Old range: $4.29 - $4.32

  • New Range: $4.31 - $4.33

  • Midpoint: $4.32 (up ~ 1.5 cents)

The revised midpoint came in slightly above analysts who were modeling for $4.31. ADC’s earnings often look like this - small beats and small upward adjustments.

Quick Note on Issuing Equity

Similar to other net lease REITs, external growth is a key part of ADC’s business model. The company issues shares to fund their acquisitions. ADC has consistently been selective about when to raise equity and when to use their "ATM forward offerings." For those curious, here’s a simple explanation:

  1. The company can lock in a price today for shares that won’t be delivered until a later date.

  2. That allows ADC to line up funding with their acquisition timing.

  3. It prevents the balance sheet from carrying large amounts of idle cash.

There can be some complications when it comes to accounting, but ADC already factors that into their AFFO guidance. It also isn’t significant enough to worry about.

Really short explanation:

  • Forward sale - price is locked now.

  • Settlement - shares are delivered and ADC gets the cash.

Shares Outstanding

ADC was very active initiating these forward sales during Q3 2024 and in October 2024. After October, ADC has slowed down their activity since they already have a large amount of shares waiting to be settled. Here’s the breakdown of Q3 2025:

  • Nearly 3.5 million shares were settled.

  • Total settled shares from the Q3 2024 program increased from 2.87 million to 6.34 million (rounded).

ADC ended the quarter with about 111.5 million weighted-average shares outstanding and still has approximately 14 million unsettled forward shares - a substantial amount for future acquisitions.

Q3 2025 Highlights

ADC had several things to highlight this quarter:

  • Invested $451 million during Q3 2025 across 110 high-quality retail net lease assets spanning 34 states.

  • 30 development or DFP (developer funding platform) projects completed or under construction for over $190 million.

  • 2.4 million square feet of leasing activity through the first nine months of 2025 with a recapture rate of 104%.

ADC continues to find attractive properties in a market that is fairly competitive. Here’s the breakdown for Q3 2025 acquisitions:

  • 90 properties

  • $401.4 million in total purchase volume

  • 7.2% cap rate

  • 10.7-year weighted-average lease term

  • 70% of ABR from investment-grade tenants

All of this information shows how consistent ADC’s long-standing strategy is: long leases, high credit quality, and strong underwriting.

Disposition Volume Guidance

ADC has revised guidance for disposition volume:

This is still a small part of the business, but slightly higher than previously expected. It’s not likely to be a material factor. The cap rate is expected to be between 7% and 8%.

Where ADC Stands Now

Agree Realty rarely has dramatic quarters. Stability is a feature of the model, not a bug. This was yet another quarter where the company had quiet, predictable executions.

  • Slight beats - check

  • Guidance nudged up - check

  • Acquisitions in order - check

  • Great balance sheet - check

Nothing in the results suggests that investors should think twice about this company. If anything, the repeated and consistent results reinforce that the business is running exactly as planned.

Treasury yields have been falling, which may impact our valuations in future quarters. Lower rates generally support a higher valuation for net lease REITs. While we didn’t do it this quarter, there's the possibility of a modest bump in price targets for next quarter.

ADC currently trades near the top of our range that we would consider fair value. Things get a bit tricky when putting a valuation on ADC. The numbers are looking great, and the strategy remains sound, but the stock price is just a bit too high.

data.title: Balancing The Risk-Reward Tradeoff: Is It Time To Move Toward Cash In Your Financial Portfolio?
data.description:
Portfolio management has to do with the management of an investor's (e.g., an individual's or a family's) financial portfolio over time in terms of risk tolerance, portfolio diversification, investment alternatives, and changing economic conditions.

As a broad starting point, investors may want to consider three or more scenarios of what the future may hold for a broad spectrum of financial assets, i.e., stocks, bonds, and everything else. Famously, Sergio Leone made a spaghetti Western movie called The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly (1966), which starred Clint Eastwood. Along these lines, one simple scenario-based approach might be to think about "best case," "base case," and "worst case" scenarios of what the economic future may hold for investors in the U.S. and around the world.

In this article, I'm interested in exploring a hypothetical worst-case scenario where one assumes that the financial markets do not currently "price in" all the risks facing the U.S. economy, such as inflation, higher unemployment leading to a slowing economy, "stagflation," the economic effects of a prolonged government shutdown and the dismantling of the U.S. government, the risk that the Federal Reserve may lose its independent status, the risk of another cryptocurrency crisis that might lead to a more general financial crisis, and a possible failure of the government to deal with the next financial crisis reasonably. This is Part 1 of a series, as I don't expect to be able to address all of the relevant issues in detail in this article.

The specific question I'd like to focus on is whether investors should "move to cash," i.e., should hold a larger proportion of their financial portfolio in cash or near-cash financial assets. For this article, I will define "cash" as financial assets that are protected by FDIC deposit insurance in the event of a bank failure. Qualified deposits are automatically insured up to at least $250,000 at each FDIC-insured bank. FDIC insurance covers deposit accounts at insured banks, such as checking, savings, money market deposit accounts (MMDAs), and certificates of deposit (CDs). The Securities Investor Protection Corporation (OTCPK:SIPC) provides insurance protection for certain non-deposit investments held in brokerage accounts.

In this article, I aim to explore how changing economic conditions may affect investors' risk tolerance, which would lead to the rebalancing of their investment portfolios. This Seeking Alpha article discusses six issues:

1. Tariffs are a tax on American consumers and businesses and a source of inconvenience, disruptions, and shortages.

Jim Puzzanghera reports that: "Tariffs are collected when foreign goods arrive at the border and are paid by the business bringing them into the country. So they amount to a back-door tax increase on U.S. businesses and consumers, and in particular hit lower-income people harder." The reality is that tariffs raise the prices of food and other imported products and/or cause shortages if importers remove those products from the U.S. market. Annie Lowrey reports that "Households will pay an average of $2,400 more for goods this year, thanks to Trump's policies." It is already evident that tariffs equal taxes and are leading to an affordability crisis in the U.S. SCOTUS may eventually overturn the tariffs based on a "separation of powers" legal argument, but, until they rule, the tariffs will continue to wreak economic havoc for American consumers and businesses.

2. Recession.

Annie Lowrey reports that "Without admitting it, the White House is pursuing a multipronged strategy to raise prices, suppress consumption, freeze production, and lower productivity in the United States." President Donald Trump has been dismantling the U.S. government, but he doesn't seem to understand that he is also dismantling the U.S. economy--once the "egg" is broken, it may not be possible to put it back together. The U.S. economy is based on the government and business working together to efficiently produce the goods and services that American consumers and businesses need. Annie Lowrey reports that Trump's policies "will raise costs and reduce supply." Counterproductive and disruptive economic policies could lead to a recession.

3. Inflation and a recession would mean stagflation.

The reality is that tariffs would likely raise the prices of imported products and/or cause shortages if importers remove those products from the U.S. market. Derek Thompson points out that, "In the 1960s, the politician Iain Macleod coined the term 'stagflation' to describe the UK's combination of faltering growth and surging prices. Like British rock, stagflation crossed the Atlantic and hit the U.S. with hurricane force. Today the term typically refers to our 1970s economy, in which a series of oil crises shut down supply chains, slowed growth, and led to a period of painful inflation." Self-imposed stagflation may result from Trump's economic policies — the U.S. won't be able to blame OPEC for stagflation this time around.

4. Another cybercurrency crisis could be a severe economic shock to the U.S. economy.

Ben McKenzie says that: "Two years ago, we saw a string of banking failures tied to crypto sending shockwaves through the financial system, exposing just how much risk banks had pooled to cater to the industry. While the worst was narrowly averted, the same conditions that sparked that crisis are not only still with us, they’ve metastasized. Thanks to an influx of deregulation and unprecedented political influence, crypto is once again primed to take down the banking system. This time, though, the crash could be even bigger—and we might not be able to bail ourselves out again." Nobel Prize Winner Jean Tirole points out that "Stablecoins arose as a supposed solution to the last of these problems....But financial innovations with an aura of safety often sow the seeds of crises, as derivatives and bundles of subprime securities did before 2008." David Frum reports that "The problem is that the new guardrails protect the profits of issuers without sufficiently reducing the risks to buyers and taxpayers. As a result, the GENIUS Act more likely ensures that the next round of stablecoin failures, when it comes, will be bigger and more destructive to the non-crypto economy." Cybercurrency has no inherent value but just trades on investors' expectations on a moment-by-moment basis. However, stablecoins are different; given the GENIUS Act, one could argue that they are safer for investors than other forms of cybercurrency, with most of their risk transferred to the U.S. financial system as a whole. State Street explains that:

It would be premature to say that stablecoins will precipitate the next financial crisis. Moreover, regulations will surely evolve as the stablecoin industry evolves. Nonetheless, there is sufficient evidence to suggest that mainstreaming stablecoins will stress-test the financial system in the coming years.

5. The Federal Reserve may lose its independence.

Pressure on the Federal Reserve in the Trump era to grow the economy and keep interest rates low presents enormous risks to the U.S. financial system. These risks would be dramatically heightened if the Fed were to lose its independence. Brookings says that "In practice, independence means that the Fed can set interest rates without interference from Congress or the White House, even if politicians are unhappy with Fed policy—and say so publicly." However, the Fed's independence could disappear in the near future. Again, given the GENIUS Act, stablecoins will present enormous challenges for U.S. regulators. Thus, Brookings explains that:

The GENIUS Act makes clear that payment stablecoins are neither a security nor a national currency, nor do they have deposit insurance or automatic access to Federal Reserve payment services. The law says that payment stablecoin issuers can be nonbank entities (federal or state qualified) or subsidiaries of insured depository institutions (IDIs). Issuers’ activities are restricted to offering and redeeming stablecoins and ancillary activities.

This article highlights four key issues for regulators as they implement the GENIUS Act:

First, financial regulators need to write capital, liquidity, and risk management requirements for issuers within 18 months to ensure stable value, and to protect financial stability and the singleness of money (to the maximum extent possible without central bank settlement).

Second, the Treasury, Federal Reserve (Fed), and Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) need to set conditions under which nonfinancial companies can issue stablecoins to avoid excessive concentration of economic power or increase financial stability risks.

Third, the Treasury needs to make sure that the “comparable regulatory regime” for foreign issuers to offer stablecoins to U.S. residents is as strong as the rules for domestic issuers to reduce arbitrage incentives.

Fourth, FinCEN, a bureau of the Treasury responsible for implementing and enforcing compliance with Anti-Money Laundering/Countering the Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT) rules, needs to write new regulations to ensure issuers have the technological capacity to counter illicit finance.

6. Unwillingness to "bail out" the economy could result in another Great Depression.

Jon Sindreu says that "During President Trump’s first term, stocks rode high on the belief that he would always pull back on policies that led to a selloff. Now, the administration is making a much tougher pitch: that even if tariffs and budget cuts cause a period of havoc, there are unexpected gains to be made on the other side. The problem is there isn’t much evidence to make investors believe that. Indeed, such views edge close to the “liquidationist” approach historically espoused by laissez-faire economists, and most infamously associated with former President Herbert Hoover’s Treasury secretary [Andrew Mellon] who advised him to let the economy fall."

The lesson of 1929 was that fiscal stimulus was needed to end the Great Depression of the 1930s. The Economist reported that, "But of course, it took the massive, deficit-funded wartime spending of the early 1940s to hammer the final nails into the coffin of the Great Depression. The lesson there, it would seem, is that where stimulus is concerned, massive is better than merely large."

CONCLUSION

Q. Should investors "move to cash," i.e., should investors hold a larger proportion of their financial portfolio in cash or near-cash financial assets?

A. I would say that the answer is yes. There are plenty of reasons why Americans may want to try to make sure that they will weather a severe financial worst-case scenario without too much hardship. The FDIC helps "maintain stability and public confidence in the U.S. financial system. One way we do this is by ensuring deposits of at least $250,000 per depositor, per ownership category, at each FDIC-insured bank."

I want to conclude that investors should do their own due diligence, including consulting with their investment advisors, before they rebalance their investment portfolios. Moving toward a larger proportion of cash or near-cash in investments may tend to reduce investors' investment risk profile, but there could be other factors to consider, including the opportunity cost associated with the possible lost opportunities to grow their investment portfolio over time.

data.title: Saudi Arabia: MBS Visit Reinforces The Thesis' Attractiveness
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying assets that track major Saudi indices such as the iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia (NYSEARCA:KSA) ETF and Franklin FTSE Saudi Arabia (NYSEARCA:FLSA) ETF. Recently, Saudi Arabian Prince Mohammad bin Salman visited President Trump at the White House, and announced major investments in the US.

Saudi Arabia is promoting great diversification of its economy, with a big boost to sectors such as tourism, infrastructure, technology and entertainment. This entire transformation is financed by its barrels of oil, and the most interesting thing is that the P/E of the ETFs that give exposure to this transformation is not yet expensive.

KSA and FLSA

I have ETF articles published recurrently on Seeking Alpha, and have been planning to write about an ETF that tracks Saudi Arabian assets for some time. Why not write in the midst of Mohammad bin Salman 's visit to the White House and President Trump?

The KSA ETF was created to expose investors to Saudi Arabian assets. The ETF currently has $671 million of AuM , and expense ratio of 0.75%. Its performance of -6% year to date also caught my attention, which I see as an opportunity.

The ETF has 169 holdings, and the main three account for more than 30% of the total composition. Although Saudi Arabia is known for its oil sector, this ETF has 40% exposure to financials , 14% to basic material, and almost 12% to energy.

Another option is the FLSA ETF, from the manager Franklin Templeton. In this case, the ETF has only $21 million in AuM, however the expense ratio is only 0.39%, that is, more attractive than the KSA.

Another highlight is that although the Franklin FTSE Saudi Arabia ETF only features 73 holdings, but the weight of the main holdings and the composition of the sectors is very similar to the KSA. This causes tracking error to be low.

In general, the retail investor tends to opt for the FLSA due to the more attractive expense ratio, while a fund manager tends to opt for the KSA due to liquidity.

The Diversification Plan

The starting point is the Vision2030 plan announced in 2016, which aims to diversify the economy and create an environment conducive to investment. The fact is that Saudi Arabia's economy has almost doubled since 2016, as we can see below, and is close to $1.3 trillion.

When Saudi leaders said in their plan that the goal was to diversify the economy, they weren't lying. Non-oil activities will reach half of GDP by 2024. This is a large economic diversification project financed by Saudi oil.

Investment Soared

Foreign investors started by observing, but gradually bought into the idea. In 2025, foreign direct investment grew 24% compared to 2024, and reached $32 billion as we can see below. The detail is that these investments are mostly directed to new sectors, not related to the oil sector.

A great example is the NEOM project, considered the “icing on the cake” of this transformational plan. The project aims to transform the Red Sea coast of northwestern Saudi Arabia into a futuristic city. See below the evolution of the island of Sindalah , one of NEOM's projects, in a photo taken in 2022 and a photo taken in 2024.

There are several projects in this sense, in addition to NEOM, we have The Line, AMAALA, Red Sea Project among others. There are billions of dollars in CapEx adding up all the projects. Another highlight is that, for example, part of the NEOM project is financed by the sovereign wealth fund PIF.

Saudi PIF

The PIF has become gigantic, with projections of reaching $2 trillion in AuM. These are investments in infrastructure, technology, entertainment, football, which give it great influence and power.

In parallel, Saudi Arabia is experiencing a major transformation, such as the female workforce, which has doubled since 2016.

Geopolitical Positioning

Here is one of the great qualities of the Saudi authorities in my opinion. Government officials negotiate with the USA, such as fighter jet purchases and technology agreements, but they also negotiate a lot with China, Israel and Iran.

In a world as marked by geopolitical conflicts as we see today, a country that can deal with such different partners displays a competitive advantage that is difficult for other nations to replicate.

Target Price

To estimate a target price, I will use the P/E multiple with the ETF KSA. As we can see below, in the last 5 years the ETF has traded at lows at 14x earnings and at highs at 21x earnings, making an average of 17.5x earnings.

The ETF currently trades at 15x earnings, so if the ETF returns and trades at the average of the last 5 years, we have an upside of 16.7% which corroborates my recommendation to buy assets that track the main Saudi indices, such as the KSA and FLSA. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio is very attractive due to the exposure to this entire economic transformation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As with all investments, we have risks relevant to this thesis. The most serious thing in my opinion is the extremely centralized power in one figure, the prince of Saudi Arabia and prime minister Mohamed bin Salman.

Although models like this can bring rapid economic advancement, there are several controversies regarding markets. An example of these controversies is China, and its control over several markets such as lithium, rare earths, among others.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's Quant tools recommend strong selling of the ETF, and this differs from my recommendation to buy due to all the arguments mentioned.

The Bottom Line

The prince of Saudi Arabia recently visited President Trump and announced investments in the United States. However, perhaps an even smarter investment is to buy assets that track Saudi indices, as I have argued in this article.

Saudi Arabia promotes a major transformation in its economy through diversification. Investors refer the country to oil, but the fact is that half of the GDP is already generated by other sectors, such as tourism, infrastructure, technology and entertainment.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main Saudi indices. In my opinion, the transformation guided by the Vision2030 plan is not currently priced in with ETFs trading at 17.5x earnings. The risk-return ratio seems very attractive.

data.title: Nvidia: Everyone Knows There's A Bubble
data.description:
It seems like everyone is talking about an AI bubble recently, and many high-quality AI stocks have been hammered relentlessly in recent weeks. General market sentiment has turned horrid, as the Fear and Greed Index hit an ultra-low level of six.

Things appear to be so terrible sentiment-wise, that we are approaching the April-lows levels, displaying one of the weakest sentient environments on record. Also, the S&P VIX Index (VIX), Wall Street's Fear Index, has seen a considerable uptick recently.

The VIX Index (Fear Gauge)

The VIX was already elevated, but the sharp post-Nvidia earnings reversal caused the VIX to surge from about 19-28 in a single session. This illustrated nearly a 50% increase in the VIX throughout the session, a reversal of this magnitude is very rare indeed. Also, just look at some of these stats from some of our favorite AI stocks.

Top AI Stocks Down From Their Highs

  • Meta Platforms (META): 28%
  • Oracle (ORCL): 40%
  • Palantir (PLTR): 27%
  • Micron (MU): 25%
  • AMD (AMD): 25%
  • Dell (DELL): 31%
  • Microsoft (MSFT): 15%.

Then, just as it seemed Nvidia (NVDA) had come to the rescue with an excellent earnings announcement, the stock market and AI stocks reversed, in one of the most violent market reversals I've seen in a long time.

Invesco "Nasdaq 100" ETF (QQQ)

QQQ had about a 4% intraday reversal after Nvidia's earnings rally faded, closing around the lows for the session, marking about an 8% pullback from recent highs.

The market has demonstrated that it wasn't done going down yet, no matter how good of a quarter and guidance Nvidia reported. After all, Nvidia reported another excellent quarter but the stock suffered about an 8% reversal from peak to trough.

Nvidia: Another Home Run

If Nvidia's Q3 earnings report isn't a home run, then I don't know what is. The company reported a double beat, topping EPS and revenues estimates. The sales beat of almost $2 billion was significant and illustrated a staggering 62.5% YoY sales gain. Data center revenue increased by 66% YoY.

Perhaps more impressively, the sales outlook for Q4 was huge. The company guided to $65B in revenues vs. the consensus figure of just about $61.84B. GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be 74.8% and 75.0%, which is still very high.

With results like these, it's challenging to see what the problem is, given that Nvidia is the leader in the AI space. After all, if things are going really well at Nvidia, the engine essentially powering AI at its base, then surely, this dynamic should be favorable for the broader AI segment, as it sends out positive ripple effects throughout the industry.

So, What Is The Market So Fearful About Now?

Well, it could be several factors, but one of the pressing issues here may be the so called circular financing bubble.

AI circular financing involves a handful of the most prominent tech companies and many of their smaller partner companies to essentially fuel the growth in the AI build out process. Chipmakers like Nvidia, AI model developers like OpenAI (OPENAI), massive cloud providers like Oracle, and other major tech firms invest billions in each other. This phenomenon has a "loop" effect as it drives buying of AI hardware, software, and services from one another on a massive scale.

Yet, some pressing questions exist, like how much AI investment is too much? Will these investments prove economically viable in the end? Also, what about the massive debt many of these companies are taking on? What will happen once the spending slows down, and when will this bubble ultimately burst?

In my view, while we're seeing some deflation in AI and momentum stocks in general, it is unlikely the end of the AI super cycle, yet. Instead, I believe we are experiencing a healthy and necessary correction process.

Also, there could be much more AI investment and M&A as the easier monetary phase potentially kicks into high gear next year, and possibly in 2027. At the same time we must be mindful that at some point the AI-bull market will end, and we could witness a significant crash then.

Another Thing Troubling Markets

The AI circular trade may be a longer term issue, but the market may be more nervous because of a more hawkish Fed now. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) crashed to about $80K recently, which is about a 35% decline from the recent highs. Bitcoin losing so much ground illustrates liquidity outflows from the ultimate risk asset market, which is spilling over into equity markets, and it's likely because of the overly hawkish Fed.

No Cut In December?

I have said that Fed Chair Jerome Powell's hawkish tone at the last FOMC meeting would be a problem. Earlier, the market was much more skeptical about a no-cut possibility in December, but now there is about a 2/3 probability that there will not be a rate cut in December.

This dynamic complicates matters in the near term, putting more pressure and creating increased uncertainty in markets, also putting the holiday/year-end rally into question.

Still, I wonder, have the rate cut probabilities tilted too bearish, and in a worst-case scenario, we may miss one rate cut, but, on the bight side, we have a much easier monetary environment to look forward to in 2026.

Wall Street Remains Bullish on Nvidia

Despite the recent volatility in Nvidia's stock, the average price target on the street is close to $250. Moreover, given the recent stellar earnings and guidance, it seems likely that price target estimates could get lifted more in future months. Also, the higher end estimates range to over $350, implying that the stock could have 40-100% upside in the next 12 months.

On the downside, the lowest estimates are around $140-150, which could also get lifted after the recent earnings announcement. This dynamic suggests that the downside may be limited in Nvidia's stock, and the risk/reward ratio is firmly skewed to the upside here.

The Bottom Line: Is Nvidia In A Bubble?

Nvidia's current forward P/E ratio is about 25, which is not terribly expensive, if the company can maintain a solid growth trajectory in the coming years. Moreover, Nvidia's forward EPS growth could average about 33% over the next several years. Therefore, Nvidia trades at a forward PEG ratio of only about 0.75 here.

A 0.75 PEG is cheap for a value company. However, given that Nvidia is still a growth company, it could have a PEG ratio of about 1.5 and still be considered relatively inexpensive. Thus, the market may be overly pessimistic regarding Nvidia and other top AI companies here. It seems probable that once the AI scare phase passes and we receive more clarity regarding future Fed policy, the market may return to driving valuations and multiples higher.

Therefore, I don't see a reason why Nvidia's stock (and other top tech stocks) couldn't increase by 50-100% over the next 12-18 months or so under the right circumstances, where we have a more accessible monitor environment coupled with continued AI growth. Due to this dynamic we are likely seeing and approaching many high quality intermediate and longer-term buying opportunities in the market now.
